Implementation Prompt: Create a public-ready Python repo named `bus-charging-scheduler`. Lay out this structure: `app.py` (Streamlit entry), `scheduler/` (engine package), `scheduler/rules/` (pluggable rules), `data/scenarios/` (scenario files), `tests/`, `requirements.txt`, `README.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `.gitignore` (Python). Add empty `__init__.py` to every package. One repo, one process — no separate services.
Acceptance Criteria: Repo tree matches the layout; `python -c 'import scheduler'` works; .gitignore excludes __pycache__, .venv, .streamlit/secrets.
Spec Ref: Stack & hosting; Deliverable 2

Implementation Prompt: Create `requirements.txt` pinned to known-good versions: streamlit, pandas. Add `ortools` ONLY if you choose the CP-SAT optimizer path (keep optional). Keep the list minimal so Streamlit Community Cloud installs cleanly from this file.
Acceptance Criteria: `pip install -r requirements.txt` succeeds in a clean venv; Streamlit Cloud build reads it automatically.
Spec Ref: Stack & hosting (requirements.txt)

Implementation Prompt: Create `scheduler/config.py` holding ONLY physical/world defaults that are not part of a scenario: default SPEED_KMPH=60, CHARGE_MINUTES=25, BATTERY_RANGE_KM=240. Expose as a frozen dataclass `WorldDefaults`. Everything else (route, stations, weights, buses) must come from the scenario file, NOT from here. Document that these are overridable per-scenario.
Acceptance Criteria: No magic numbers anywhere else in the codebase; grep for 25/240/60 returns only config.py.
Spec Ref: Physical constants; tunable/no-hardcode mandate

Implementation Prompt: Create `README.md` with section stubs: Overview, Live link, Run locally, Project layout, How to change a weight, How to add a new rule, Scenarios, Assumptions. Fill prose later (H1).
Acceptance Criteria: All required README sections present as headers.
Spec Ref: Deliverable: README.md

Implementation Prompt: Create `ARCHITECTURE.md` with stubs: Framework choice & why, Data structure design, Anticipated future changes (table), Change a weight (code example), Add a rule (code example), Assumptions. Fill later (H2-H6).
Acceptance Criteria: All required ARCHITECTURE sections present as headers.
Spec Ref: Deliverable: ARCHITECTURE.md

Implementation Prompt: In `scheduler/model.py` define a `Segment(from_node, to_node, distance_km)` dataclass and a `Route(nodes: list[str], segments: list[Segment])`. Provide `distance_between(node_a, node_b)` and `cumulative_distance(node)`. Route must be data-driven (read from scenario), so changing a segment distance or adding a node is data-only.
Acceptance Criteria: Route built from a list of nodes+distances; querying B->C returns 100; adding node 'E' needs no code change.
Spec Ref: Route table; 'change a segment distance' curveball

Implementation Prompt: Define `Station(id, node, num_chargers=1)`. Store stations as data on the scenario, keyed by node. `num_chargers` MUST be a field (today=1) so 'double the chargers' is a data edit. Only intermediate nodes (A,B,C,D) are stations; endpoints are not.
Acceptance Criteria: Station carries num_chargers; setting num_chargers=2 requires no engine code change (see C7).
Spec Ref: 1 charger per station; 'double the chargers' curveball

Implementation Prompt: Define operators as a simple set/registry derived from buses, plus an optional `Operator(id, attributes={})` for future per-operator config (priority, SLA). Do not hardcode the 3 names; derive from data.
Acceptance Criteria: Operators discovered from bus list; adding 'newop' is data-only.
Spec Ref: 3 operators; 'swap in a new operator' curveball

Implementation Prompt: Define `Bus(id, operator, direction, departure_min, origin, range_km=None, priority=0)`. `direction` is derived from origin/destination via the route, not an enum hardcoded to two values. `range_km` defaults to scenario battery range but is per-bus overridable. `priority` field reserved for future priority-bus rule.
Acceptance Criteria: Bus supports per-bus range & priority fields even though scenarios don't use them yet.
Spec Ref: Buses table; priority buses & variable range (future)

Implementation Prompt: Design the scenario file as JSON (or YAML). Top-level keys: `name`, `world` (speed, charge_minutes, battery_range), `route` (nodes + segment distances), `stations` (node -> num_chargers), `weights` (individual, operator, overall + open dict for future weights), `buses` (list). This single file fully describes a situation. Write `data/scenarios/scenario_1.json` as the canonical example and document the schema inline in ARCHITECTURE.
Acceptance Criteria: A scenario file alone fully specifies the world; reviewer can read it and understand the input.
Spec Ref: 'A scenario IS your data structure'; design data first

Implementation Prompt: Represent weights as a dict `{individual:1.0, operator:1.0, overall:1.0, ...}` loaded from the scenario, NOT constants. The cost function reads weights by key, so adding a new weighted term = add a key + a rule, no engine edit. Provide a default-merge so missing keys fall back to 1.0.
Acceptance Criteria: Changing operator weight to 2.0 is a one-value edit in the scenario JSON; new weight keys are accepted without code change.
Spec Ref: Tunable weights, one obvious place, not hardcoded

Implementation Prompt: Define output dataclasses: `ChargeEvent(station, arrive_min, start_min, wait_min, end_min)`, `BusPlan(bus_id, charge_events:list, arrival_min, total_wait)`, `ScheduleResult(bus_plans:list, station_order:dict[node->list[bus_id]], objective_breakdown:dict)`. This is what the UI renders.
Acceptance Criteria: Result object carries everything the per-bus and per-station views need; serializable for debugging.
Spec Ref: Output: timeline, waits, arrival, station order

Implementation Prompt: Write `scheduler/loader.py: load_scenario(path) -> Scenario`. Parse JSON into model objects, merge world defaults, validate: distances>0, every bus operator non-empty, departure>=0, route connected, station nodes ⊆ route intermediate nodes. Raise clear errors. Add `list_scenarios(dir)` for the dropdown.
Acceptance Criteria: All 5 files load; a malformed file raises a readable ValueError; list_scenarios returns 5 names.
Spec Ref: Reads any scenario from your data files

Implementation Prompt: In `scheduler/physics.py`: `travel_minutes(distance_km, speed_kmph)`, `arrival_time_at(node, bus, route, speed)` returning clock minutes a bus reaches each node assuming no waiting yet. Use consistent speed from world config. Pure functions, fully unit-testable.
Acceptance Criteria: 60 km/h over 100 km returns 100 min; arrival at A for a 19:00 BK bus = departure+100.
Spec Ref: Travel time from distance; consistent speed

Implementation Prompt: In `scheduler/plans.py: candidate_plans(bus, route, range_km) -> list[list[node]]`. Enumerate ordered subsets of the bus's downstream stations such that no leg (start->first, between charges, last->dest) exceeds range_km, and stations are visited in route order (no backtracking). Return all feasible station-subset plans for the scheduler to choose among.
Acceptance Criteria: BK bus (540 km, 240 range) yields only plans with >=2 charges; every returned plan respects the 240 km leg constraint; KB direction enumerates its own station order.
Spec Ref: 240km rule; >=2 charges for BK; route order; no backtrack

Implementation Prompt: Create `scheduler/rules/base.py` with an abstract `Rule` (name, kind in {'hard','soft'}, `def evaluate(context) -> float|bool`). Hard rules return feasibility (bool/inf cost); soft rules return a non-negative penalty. Create a `RuleRegistry` that auto-discovers rule instances. Engine consumes the registry generically — adding a rule = drop a file + register, NO engine edit.
Acceptance Criteria: A new rule file added to rules/ is picked up automatically; engine code does not mention specific rule names.
Spec Ref: Add a new rule without rewriting the engine

Implementation Prompt: As Rule subclasses: `RangeRule` (no leg > range), `ChargerExclusivityRule` (<= num_chargers buses overlapping at a station), `ChargeDurationRule` (exactly charge_minutes), `RouteOrderRule` (no backtracking). Each is independently testable and registered as kind='hard'.
Acceptance Criteria: Each hard rule flags the corresponding violation in a crafted bad schedule; valid schedule passes all.
Spec Ref: All four hard rules that must always hold

Implementation Prompt: As Rule subclasses kind='soft': `IndividualWaitRule` (penalty ~ per-bus wait/lateness), `OperatorRule` (penalty ~ variance/total wait within each operator fleet), `OverallRule` (penalty ~ network total time / makespan). Each multiplies its penalty by the matching weight read from scenario weights by key.
Acceptance Criteria: Setting operator weight 2.0 measurably increases operator term's influence; each rule reads its own weight key.
Spec Ref: 3 soft rules; tunable weights drive behavior

Implementation Prompt: In `scheduler/objective.py: score(schedule, registry, weights) -> (feasible:bool, total_cost:float, breakdown:dict)`. Sum soft penalties * weights; if any hard rule fails -> infeasible. Return per-term breakdown for transparency/debugging and for ARCHITECTURE evidence.
Acceptance Criteria: Breakdown sums to total_cost; infeasible schedule reports which hard rule failed.
Spec Ref: Weigh three soft rules; defensible scoring

Implementation Prompt: Model each station as a resource with `num_chargers` time-slots of length charge_minutes. `reserve(station, requested_start) -> actual_start` assigns the earliest free charger >= requested_start, computing wait. Generalize over num_chargers so 1 vs 2 chargers is data-only. This resolves 'who goes first, who waits'.
Acceptance Criteria: With 1 charger, overlapping requests serialize (second waits); with num_chargers=2 two buses charge concurrently — no code change, only data.
Spec Ref: One bus per charger; decide order & waits; 'double chargers'

Implementation Prompt: Implement the chosen approach in `scheduler/engine.py: schedule(scenario) -> ScheduleResult`. RECOMMENDED: deterministic, event-driven greedy — process charge requests in time order; for each bus pick the feasible candidate plan + charger ordering that minimizes the weighted objective (C6) given commitments so far; tie-break by bus priority then departure then id. Keep the search strategy swappable (Strategy pattern) so CP-SAT/ILP can replace greedy later without touching rules or data.
Acceptance Criteria: Returns a complete ScheduleResult for all buses; deterministic across runs; swapping strategy class doesn't touch rules.
Spec Ref: Decides plans + order; approach must be defensible & scalable

Implementation Prompt: From engine commitments, build each `BusPlan`: arrival at each station, charge start (after wait), wait minutes, charge end, departure, and final arrival at destination. Compose into ScheduleResult.bus_plans.
Acceptance Criteria: Each bus timeline is internally consistent (end=start+charge_minutes; next leg arrival = prev end + travel).
Spec Ref: Compute per-bus timeline: charge where/when, wait, arrival

Implementation Prompt: Populate `station_order[node] = [bus_id ...]` sorted by actual charge start time at each station, including which charger index when multi-charger. This feeds the per-station view.
Acceptance Criteria: Order at each station matches charge start times; consistent with bus timelines.
Spec Ref: Per-station view: order buses charged

Implementation Prompt: After scheduling, run all hard rules over the final ScheduleResult and assert zero violations (defensive double-check). Expose `validate(result, scenario) -> list[Violation]`. Surface any violation loudly (the schedule must never silently break the 240 km rule).
Acceptance Criteria: validate() returns [] for all 5 scenarios; deliberately corrupted result returns specific violations.
Spec Ref: Correctness: schedules respect all hard rules

Implementation Prompt: Define a clean function boundary: UI/CLI calls `load_scenario(path)` then `schedule(scenario)`. No Streamlit imports inside scheduler package (engine must be usable headless / from tests / future REST API).
Acceptance Criteria: Engine importable and runnable with zero Streamlit dependency; `python -m scheduler.engine data/scenarios/scenario_1.json` prints a result.
Spec Ref: Scalability: engine reusable beyond this UI

Implementation Prompt: Provide adapters converting ScheduleResult into pandas DataFrames the UI needs: `to_bus_table(result)`, `to_station_table(result, node)`, `to_input_table(scenario)`. Keep formatting (HH:MM) in the adapter, not the engine.
Acceptance Criteria: Adapters return tidy DataFrames; engine stays format-agnostic (minutes only).
Spec Ref: UI renders per-bus & per-station; input table

Implementation Prompt: Wrap scenario load + schedule in `st.cache_data` keyed by scenario name + weights so re-selecting a scenario is instant and recompute only on change.
Acceptance Criteria: Switching scenarios twice triggers compute once; changing a weight invalidates cache.
Spec Ref: Smooth UX; scalability

Implementation Prompt: In `app.py`: set page config, title, and a `st.selectbox` populated from `list_scenarios()` AT THE TOP so reviewers land on the dropdown immediately. Load the selected scenario and run the scheduler (cached). Use tabs/sections for the three views.
Acceptance Criteria: Opening the app shows the dropdown first; selecting a scenario triggers a scheduled result.
Spec Ref: Dropdown at top; land on app & see it immediately

Implementation Prompt: Add a section showing the raw input: a readable table of buses (id, operator, direction, departure HH:MM) plus the world/route/stations/weights summary. Optionally show raw JSON in an expander so reviewers see exactly what's fed in.
Acceptance Criteria: Reviewer can see the full input for any scenario, including the active weights.
Spec Ref: Scenario view showing the input

Implementation Prompt: Render one row (or expandable block) per bus: stations used, arrive/start/wait/end at each, and final arrival time, all HH:MM. Show charge count. Sort by departure. Make waits visually obvious (e.g., highlight non-zero waits).
Acceptance Criteria: Every bus shows a complete, readable timeline; BK buses show >=2 charges; waits are visible.

Implementation Prompt: For each of A,B,C,D show the ordered list of buses that charged there (bus id, operator, charge start, wait, end, charger index if multi). One table or column per station.
Acceptance Criteria: Order at each station is clear and matches the schedule; reviewer can sanity-check fairness vs weights.
Spec Ref: Per-station view: order buses charged

Implementation Prompt: Add sidebar sliders for individual/operator/overall weights, defaulting to the scenario's values. On change, re-run the scheduler so reviewers can SEE weights change the schedule (esp. Scenario 4). Provide a 'reset to scenario defaults' button. Keep scenario file as source of truth for defaults.
Acceptance Criteria: Dragging operator weight up visibly reorders Scenario 4; reset restores file defaults.
Spec Ref: Different weights -> different defensible schedules

Implementation Prompt: Create `data/scenarios/scenario_1..5.json` from the PDF tables. S1 even 15-min spacing; S2 bunched 8-min then spaced; S3 asymmetric (10 BK, 4 KB); S4 operator-heavy (KPN 8/10) with weights individual=1,operator=2,overall=1; S5 worst-case convergence 8-min both ends. All share route/world/240km/25min/1 charger; S1-3,S5 weights default 1/1/1.
Acceptance Criteria: All 5 files parse; departure times & operators exactly match the PDF; S4 carries operator=2.0.

Implementation Prompt: `tests/test_rules.py`: each hard rule flags its violation; each soft rule returns higher penalty for worse schedules; weight multiplication correct.

Implementation Prompt: `tests/test_charger.py`: across a full schedule, no station has more concurrent charges than num_chargers; multi-charger config allows concurrency.

Implementation Prompt: Fill README: one-line overview, live Streamlit link, `streamlit run app.py` instructions, venv + pip steps, project layout, exactly how to change a weight (point to scenario JSON + sidebar), how to add a rule (point to rules/ + 5-line example), scenario list, assumptions link.

Implementation Prompt: Explain the chosen approach (event-driven greedy + pluggable weighted rule registry, Strategy-swappable to CP-SAT). Justify fit: small instances, need for easy rule addition and weight tuning, deterministic & explainable output, no heavy solver dependency. Contrast with pure ILP/CP-SAT (more optimal, heavier, harder to extend rules ad hoc) and note migration path.
Acceptance Criteria: Reviewer understands why this approach fits and how it scales; trade-offs are explicit.
Spec Ref: Docs: framework & why it's the right fit

Implementation Prompt: Write the change-foresight table: for each anticipated change state how the design absorbs it WITHOUT code changes. Cover: add a station; change a segment distance; double/var chargers per station; swap/add operator; add/remove buses; new route / multiple routes sharing stations; priority buses; time-of-day electricity cost; driver shifts; partial/variable charge time; variable speed/traffic; per-bus battery range; new soft or hard rule; new weighted objective term; bigger fleets (perf).
Acceptance Criteria: Breadth & specificity is high; each row names the exact field/file that changes (data) vs claims 'no engine rewrite'.
Spec Ref: KEY SIGNAL: breadth of anticipated changes

Implementation Prompt: Show the literal diff: edit `weights.operator` from 1.0 to 2.0 in the scenario JSON (and/or sidebar slider). Explain the cost function reads weights by key, no code touched.

Spec Ref: Docs: how to change a weight w/ example

Implementation Prompt: Show a full minimal new rule (e.g., `ElectricityCostRule` penalizing charging during a peak window) as a ~10-line Rule subclass dropped into rules/, plus the one-line weight key added to scenarios. Demonstrate the engine needs no edit.
Acceptance Criteria: A reader could paste the example and have a working new soft rule.
Spec Ref: Docs: how to add a rule w/ example; live add-rule test

Implementation Prompt: Document every assumption: speed=60 km/h; charging always to full & exactly 25 min; buses don't charge at endpoints; instantaneous decision/no re-planning mid-trip; ties broken by priority->departure->id; objective definitions for each soft term; what 'operator runs smoothly' means quantitatively; behavior if no feasible plan exists; whether buses may skip optional charges. Be honest about what's not done.
Acceptance Criteria: Assumptions are explicit and defensible; gaps in the spec are addressed.
Spec Ref: Make your own calls; document assumptions
